name: Yes, And Circle
description: Players stand in a circle and tell a story one line at a time. First time, a player says "Yes, and..." then adds their own bit to the story.
notes: After the first round, drop saying "Yes, and..." Correct students immediately if someone says "no" or "but".

name: Word Association Chain
description: Players stand in a circle. One player says a word, the next player says an associated word, and so on.
notes: Note exceptionally clever word associations that satisfy the other players. After going around the circle at least once, try repeating everything in reverse to build short-term memory recall. Can also be played like Categories.

name: Common Ground
description: Players discover shared and opposing preferences while practicing observation and risk-taking.
notes: Players directly learn about each other and indirectly learn that bold choices are more fun, even when assumptions are wrong.

name: I Like Biscuits
description: Arrange players in a circle. Say a random phrase (such as "I Like Biscuits!") while walking across the circle and maintaining eye contact with another player to pass focus. Players attempt to mirror the phrase and action as exactly as possible.
notes: The goal is to copy exactly what you see and allow your own natural physicality to influence the action. It's funniest when you aren't trying to be funny. Best played in a safe environment where mistakes are celebrated.

name: New Choice
description: Players learn to adapt quickly to a new reality. During a scene, the director can call "New Choice!" and players must immediately replace what they just said or did.
notes: Useful for encouraging flexibility and maintaining a safe classroom. If someone says something inappropriate, immediately call "New Choice!" This game works especially well after several rounds of Categories.

name: Categories
description: Players stand in a circle or line. The leader announces a category and players rapidly name items that fit it, either taking turns around the circle or speaking while the leader points at them. Decide ahead of time whether repeated answers are allowed.
notes: This game captures the spirit of saying 'and' in improv. Play it early and often. It also makes an excellent warm-up before Jump-Out games.
